content: Magic in Aefract is called Refraction: the act of borrowing from one of the Seven Antediluvians — primordial principles born when the Tear ripped open the sky and let raw creative force pour into reality. The Tear is still open; its glow is the Sky's Scar, visible from every continent. Safe Paths (Glimmer, Warmth, Knotting, Trace, Stitch) borrow with the flow and cost only mild strain — a headache, a nosebleed, a metallic taste. Forbidden Spells (Unmaking, Soul-Sight, Time-Hollowing, Raising the Dead, the Heart-Tear) borrow against the flow, and the two Antediluvian principles in conflict take payment from the caster's own body. Overcasting causes the Starving Condition: the caster's body starts leaking borrowed territory and, past a certain point, stops being a person. The theory explaining any of this is the Mechanism of Refraction — and it admits it might be wrong. The Ashveil lineage founded the first academies teaching restraint, a lost technique called the Redirected Whiplash once promised to shift a spell's cost onto a proxy, and each Antediluvian Glimmer marks a place where a Fundamental's influence bleeds visibly into the land.
name: HUB — Refraction & the Tear (constant)

content: Before all things there was the Void. Then the Tear ripped open — not by force or will, simply was — and raw creative force poured through, becoming reality. The Tear never closed. It hangs overhead as a jagged gash of faint, ancient glow: the Sky's Scar, visible from every continent, never fully dark even at midnight. No one knows what the light is. The world exists in a river flowing from an open wound — without it, reality would starve. Aethironi folklore says the Scar is a reminder not to forget what lies beyond; Veluni say it's held open by the weight of the sea and will close when the Great Current turns, ending Aefract; Iskari call it a hunt, the light of something pursued across the heavens; Kethi nomads whisper it's an eye, something watching through it. Three Guild expeditions have independently reported a vast, slow shape crossing the Scar's face — dismissed as illusion each time. Commoners call it the Sleeper and warn their children: it sleeps, do not wake it.
name: The Tear & the Sky's Scar

content: Seven formless beings crawled out of the Tear as the first consciousnesses to exist — not gods, not demons, just curious. Their curiosity diverged, and each crystallized into a fundamental principle, an invisible territory locked in a silent cold war with the other six. All of reality — matter, people, civilizations — exists in the wounded overlap where the seven territories crash together. The Vow (binding, gravity, love) opposes the Veil (distance, space, privacy). The Anchor (structural integrity, identity) opposes the Unmaking (entropy, decay). The Flame (change, ambition) opposes the Radiance (connection, empathy). The Body (matter, flesh) is the battlefield all of them fight over. Refraction is the act of borrowing from one principle to produce an effect; a spell that borrows with the flow is safe, one that borrows against it triggers a conflict the caster's own body must settle. In AB 219 three of the seven territories (the Veil, the Anchor, the Unmaking) briefly bled visibly into the known world in an event called the Rift-Tide — the first confirmed sign the Antediluvians are active and aware the known world exists.
key: /\b(Antediluvian|Antediluvians|Seven\ Antediluvians|Fundamentals|the\ Vow|the\ Veil|the\ Anchor|the\ Unmaking|the\ Flame|the\ Radiance|the\ Body|Rift\-Tide)\b/gi

name: The Seven Antediluvians
keywordsRaw: /\b(Antediluvian|Antediluvians|Seven\ Antediluvians|Fundamentals|the\ Vow|the\ Veil|the\ Anchor|the\ Unmaking|the\ Flame|the\ Radiance|the\ Body|Rift\-Tide)\b/gi

content: Refraction's practiced, repeatable core is five Safe Paths, taught to every mage before anything else. Glimmer produces a faint light. Warmth raises temperature in a small area. Knotting binds objects or rope with invisible force. Trace follows a person's or animal's recent path. Stitch closes minor wounds. Each leaves a small, harmless residue — a headache, a nosebleed, a metallic taste, tiredness — that fades within days. The one universal rule every mage lineage teaches: never attempt a spell you haven't been taught. The Rule of the Nosebleed governs everything — stop before it hurts, and if it hurts again, never try it again. Pushing past strain accumulates residue that doesn't fade; enough of it tips a caster into the Starving Condition.
name: Safe Paths & strain basics

content: Forbidden Spells always cause harm, no matter the caster's skill — forbidden by consequence, not law. Unmaking disintegrates an object completely; the caster's own hand or arm withers over a week. Soul-Sight reads a person's thoughts; the caster loses a sense, or goes mad. Time-Hollowing speeds or slows time for a target; the caster ages by the same amount they stole or gave. Raising the Dead reanimates a corpse wrong — something else moves in it — and costs the caster a piece of memory or identity each time. The Heart-Tear stops a heart from a distance; the effect usually mirrors back and stops the caster's own. The Unspoken Name — speaking the true name of one of the Seven Antediluvians — has no known survivor. Every safe spell known today represents roughly forty deaths finding its boundaries; every forbidden spell represents about three survivors who watched someone else die trying it and didn't repeat the attempt.
name: Forbidden Spells

content: What happens when a mage pushes past the body's limits, in four stages. Stage One, the Hunger: a persistent craving for raw creative force that casting only briefly relieves — reversible with weeks of rest. Stage Two, the Leak: the body sheds borrowed territory into the environment (a faint glow, wilting or hyper-growing plants nearby, agitated animals); partially reversible, but permanent traces remain. Stage Three, the Shift: physical transformation begins (glowing eyes, doubled voice, patchy skin) and the original self starts receding; irreversible, usually ends in confinement or suicide. Stage Four, the Hollowing: the person is gone. What remains, a Starving One, hungers constantly for raw creative force from living things, leaving desiccated husks behind; nearly impossible to kill by normal means; Guild protocol is termination, no exceptions. A documented Stage Five exists — 'the Bridge,' where a Starving One becomes a permanent conduit pouring borrowed territory into Aefract — but the Guild has suppressed the only known case, a rogue mage named Doran Ash who became the source of a still-sealed 40km² dead zone called the Scarlands Bleed.
key: /\b(Starving\ Condition|Starving\ One|Starving\ Ones|the\ Hunger|the\ Leak|the\ Shift|the\ Hollowing|the\ Bridge)\b/gi
keysRaw: /\b(Starving\ Condition|Starving\ One|Starving\ Ones|the\ Hunger|the\ Leak|the\ Shift|the\ Hollowing|the\ Bridge)\b/gi
name: The Starving Condition (four stages)
keywordsRaw: /\b(Starving\ Condition|Starving\ One|Starving\ Ones|the\ Hunger|the\ Leak|the\ Shift|the\ Hollowing|the\ Bridge)\b/gi

content: Before organized magic, the Age of Burning: people began manifesting Refraction with no warning and no teachers, and most who survived a first cast were killed by their own villages as witches or worshipped as gods — neither fate kind. The first mages who lived were the ones who learned restraint through trial, error, and the deaths of everyone who didn't. Ashveil — surname only, first name deliberately erased from every record — was the first to gather the manifesting outcasts instead of leaving them to die alone, teaching survival by voice rather than writing anything down. Ashveil died holding a ridge at the Cinder Field against a soul-hound pack with thirty-seven followers, buying time for villagers to flee; all thirty-eight died. Every academy still opens with the Ashveil Oath: 'I am not chosen. I am one who chose. I will keep going.' A sealed Fifth Academy scroll claims Ashveil attempted the Unspoken Name and survived three breaths before dying at the Field — the Guild dismisses it as legend and has never opened the scroll to check.
key: /\b(Ashveil|first\ mage|First\ Mages|Age\ of\ Burning|Cinder\ Field|Ashveil\ Oath)\b/gi

content: A lost, half-mythical technique known only from a single scrawled fragment: 'The debt must be paid. The vessel does not have to be me.' The theory: every spell creates a debt paid by the nearest vessel able to contain it; if a prepared living proxy is placed between caster and target, the debt flows to the proxy instead of the caster. No notebooks, diagrams, or results survive — only a bunker carving, a denied quartermaster's requisition for 'animal stock,' and a folk tale about a man who made his dog sick instead of himself. The original Redirector's fate is unknown; the leading theory is they died from an unstable binding or the moral weight of the discovery. A handful of Reformist scholars are now trying to reconstruct it from the Mechanism of Refraction theory, calling their version Whiplash Through Proxy Reformed. No one has tested it. The Guild's official position: 'The Guild strongly advises against attempting to reconstruct a technique that killed its only known practitioner.'
name: Redirected Whiplash (lost technique)

content: Beyond the abstract cold war, the Antediluvians leave fingerprints on real geography — though scholars disagree whether folk names like the Mirror, the Cage, and the Sleeper map onto the seven canonical Fundamentals. The Northeast Anchor of Stone grows unbreakable buildings and stubborn, change-resistant people. The Western Waste rots wood in months and unravels spells mid-cast, expanding a mile per decade. The Sunken Remnants run 'thin' — reflections move independently, and mages who sleep in the affected waters wake with memories that aren't their own. The Singing Wastes mute all Refractive light, a safe haven for mages clearing Cinder marks. The volcanic Sunken Spires burn with unnatural colors and have swallowed three Guild expeditions. The Deep Crater at the Scar's center shares lucid dreams for 500 miles and pulls sleepwalkers toward it — the Blind Seers of Vigil call it the Sleeper's heartbeat. The Crown and Guild suppress the only complete map, the Glimmer Atlas; the Reformists circulate stolen fragments. A new map reportedly shows all seven manifestations converging on one point beneath the Scar — suggesting it's a weakening seal, not a wound.
key: /\b(Antediluvian\ Glimmer|Glimmer\ Atlas|the\ Mirror|the\ Cage|the\ Sleeper\ region|Deep\ Crater)\b/gi
